evals.prompt:
Can you review this PRD? It's an early draft, pre-peer review. We're a B2B SaaS company building project management software for agencies. No specific concerns — just give me the full review.

---
**PRD: Team Workload View**

**Overview**
We want to build a workload view so managers can see what their team is working on. This is a commonly requested feature and will help with resource management.

**Problem**
Managers don't have visibility into what their team is working on. This causes problems with resource allocation.

**Proposed Solution**
A new view in the product that shows all active tasks assigned to each team member. Managers can see who is overloaded and who has capacity. They can reassign tasks from this view.

**Features**
- List of team members with their assigned tasks
- Visual indicator for workload (low/medium/high)
- Ability to drag and drop tasks between team members
- Filter by project, date range, or task status
- Export to CSV

**Success Metrics**
- Feature adoption
- User satisfaction

**Out of Scope**
- Time tracking integration (future)

**Timeline**
Q3
---
evals.expected_output: A full three-reviewer panel with rubric scores per criterion, a summary scorecard table, top 5 prioritized edits, a synthesis paragraph, and an offer to rewrite sections.
expectations: All three reviewers (Peer PM, Lead/Group PM, CPO) provide rubric scores for each criterion
The Peer PM flags that success metrics ('feature adoption', 'user satisfaction') are vague and not measurable
The Peer PM flags missing edge cases (empty states, no tasks assigned, large teams)
The Lead/Group PM flags missing strategic alignment — no OKR or bet referenced
The CPO questions whether the problem statement is evidence-backed (no data cited on how many managers have this problem or how often)
A scorecard table is included with per-reviewer overall scores
Top 5 prioritized edits are listed and ordered by impact
A synthesis paragraph gives an honest read on the PRD's readiness
Offer to rewrite sections is made at the end

evals.prompt:
Please review this PRD. It's ready for peer review before I take it to my Group PM. We're a fintech company — this is a feature for our expense management product used by finance teams at mid-market companies.

One thing to note: my Group PM, David, tends to be very skeptical about scope. He always says 'what's the v0 of this?' and pushes hard on whether we've considered just solving it with configuration rather than building new UI. He also always asks if we've checked with legal and compliance before putting anything on the roadmap. Use David's voice for the Lead PM reviewer.

---
**PRD: Bulk Receipt Matching**

**Problem Statement**
Finance teams at mid-market companies spend an average of 4.2 hours per week manually matching receipts to expense line items. Based on discovery interviews with 12 finance managers across 8 customers, 9 of 12 said receipt reconciliation was their top pain point in our product. Current state: users upload receipts one at a time and match them manually to transactions. There is no bulk workflow.

**Why Now**
This is blocking 3 enterprise deals in the pipeline (combined ARR: $380K). Sales has escalated twice in Q2. Competitive gap: Expensify and Ramp both support bulk matching.

**Proposed Solution**
A bulk receipt matching workflow that allows finance admins to upload multiple receipts at once and use AI-assisted matching to auto-suggest matches based on amount, date, and vendor name. Users review and confirm suggestions before they're applied.

**User Flow**
1. Admin navigates to Receipts tab
2. Selects 'Bulk Upload' (new)
3. Uploads up to 50 receipts at once (PDF or image)
4. System processes and returns match suggestions within 60 seconds
5. Admin reviews suggestions in a side-by-side view (receipt + proposed transaction)
6. Admin confirms, rejects, or manually overrides each match
7. Confirmed matches are applied; unmatched receipts flagged for manual review

**Success Metrics**
- Reduce average time-to-reconcile from 4.2 hrs/week to <1.5 hrs/week (measured via session data, 90-day post-launch)
- 70% of uploaded receipts auto-matched with >90% accuracy
- Feature adopted by 60% of eligible accounts within 60 days of GA

**Non-Goals (v1)**
- Email forwarding of receipts (v2)
- Mobile upload (v2)
- ERP sync (separate initiative)

**Open Questions**
- What's the fallback if AI match confidence is low? (Proposed: flag for manual review, don't auto-apply)
- Do we need audit logging for matched receipts for compliance purposes? (Needs legal sign-off)
- 50 receipt limit: is this right? Need to validate with power users.

**Dependencies**
- ML platform team for model training and serving
- Legal/Compliance review on audit trail requirements
- Design: new bulk upload UI pattern (no existing component)

**Timeline**
- Discovery complete: done
- Design: 3 weeks
- Eng build: 6 weeks
- QA + beta: 2 weeks
- GA: Q4
---
evals.expected_output: A full panel review where the Lead/Group PM reviewer uses David's voice — skeptical of scope, asks about configuration alternatives, pushes on legal/compliance. All reviewers score per rubric. Scorecard, top 5 edits, synthesis, offer to rewrite.
expectations: All three reviewers provide rubric scores per criterion
The Lead/Group PM reviewer clearly reflects David's voice: pushes on scope ('what's the v0?'), asks whether configuration could solve this instead of new UI, raises legal/compliance explicitly
The Peer PM acknowledges the strong parts (problem statement, user flow, open questions) while finding specific gaps
The CPO gives a view on the strategic fit and pipeline/ARR framing
A per-reviewer scorecard table is included
Summary scorecard across all three reviewers is included
Top 5 prioritized edits reference specific sections from the PRD
Synthesis gives an honest read on whether this is ready for the Group PM
Offer to rewrite is made

evals.prompt:
I need a full PRD review — this is going to my CPO next week and I want to stress test it first. We're building developer tooling, specifically a VS Code extension for an AI code review product. Our users are engineering teams at Series B–D startups.

My CPO, Priya, is very narrative-focused. She always says 'I need to be able to explain this in one sentence to the board.' She pushes on 'why us, why now' and often asks whether we're solving a product problem or a go-to-market problem. She doesn't care about implementation details in a PRD review — she cares about the bet. Use Priya's voice for the CPO reviewer.

---
**PRD: Inline Fix Suggestions (v1)**

**Strategic Context**
Our AI code review product currently surfaces issues in a side panel. Adoption data shows that 68% of flagged issues are never resolved — engineers acknowledge the feedback but don't act on it. Exit interviews with 4 churned accounts cited 'too much friction to act on suggestions' as a primary reason. This feature directly addresses that churn signal.

**Problem Statement**
Engineers see code review feedback but don't act on it because applying fixes requires context-switching: read suggestion in panel, understand the change, go to the file, find the line, make the edit. For experienced engineers, this overhead feels insulting. For junior engineers, the gap between suggestion and implementation is often too large to bridge without help.

**Proposed Solution**
Inline fix suggestions that appear directly in the editor at the line(s) flagged, with a one-click 'Apply Fix' action. The AI generates a concrete code change, not just a description of the problem. The engineer can accept, edit, or dismiss.

**Why This, Why Now**
- Activation metric (issues resolved / issues surfaced) is at 32%. Target is 60%.
- Three enterprise prospects have explicitly asked for this in the last 60 days
- GitHub Copilot has not shipped inline review fix suggestions yet — 6-month window before they likely close this gap

**User Flow**
1. Engineer opens a file with active review comments
2. Flagged lines show a gutter icon (non-intrusive)
3. Engineer hovers or clicks → inline diff appears showing the suggested fix
4. Engineer accepts (applies change), edits (opens in editor), or dismisses
5. Accepted fixes are logged; dismissed fixes prompt optional one-click reason

**Success Metrics**
- Issues resolved / issues surfaced: from 32% → 55% within 90 days of GA (primary)
- Time from issue surfaced to resolved: baseline TBD, target 40% reduction
- Fix acceptance rate: >45% of surfaced inline suggestions accepted within 7 days

**Non-Goals (v1)**
- Multi-file fixes (v2)
- Fix suggestions in PR comments / GitHub UI (separate initiative)
- Custom fix style rules per team (v2)

**Risks**
- AI fix quality: if suggestions are wrong or noisy, engineers will dismiss and disengage. Mitigation: confidence threshold — only surface suggestions above X% confidence; measure dismissal rate as a leading indicator.
- Performance: inline rendering at scale in large files. Eng has flagged this as a concern. Mitigation: lazy rendering, only render visible lines.
- Copilot shipping first: monitor. If they ship in the next 90 days, reassess positioning.

**Dependencies**
- AI platform: model fine-tuning for fix generation (6 weeks, already scoped)
- VS Code extension team: UI work (4 weeks)
- Design: inline diff component (2 weeks, new pattern)

**Open Questions**
- What's the right confidence threshold for surfacing suggestions? (Need eval data — tracking)
- Do we show suggestions for all issue types, or only a subset in v1? (Proposed: only style and correctness issues, not security)
- How do we measure fix quality post-launch without burdening engineers with surveys?

**Timeline**
- Design + AI eval: 3 weeks
- Eng: 6 weeks
- Beta (10 accounts): 2 weeks
- GA: 11 weeks from kickoff
---
evals.expected_output: A full panel review where the CPO reviewer uses Priya's voice — narrative-focused, pushes on 'why us why now', distinguishes product vs. GTM problems. All reviewers score the rubric. Scorecard, top 5 edits, synthesis, offer to rewrite.
expectations: All three reviewers provide rubric scores per criterion
The CPO reviewer reflects Priya's voice: asks whether this can be explained in one sentence to the board, pushes on 'why us why now', distinguishes product problem from GTM problem
The Peer PM engages with the strong technical flow while finding specific gaps (e.g., baseline for time-to-resolve is listed as TBD)
The Lead/Group PM assesses the competitive window claim and risks section
Per-reviewer and summary scorecards are included
Top 5 prioritized edits are specific to this PRD
Synthesis gives a clear read on whether this is ready for a CPO review
Offer to rewrite is made
